title: Helm Release Health Monitoring
app: Helm CLI / Flux helm-controller logs, `splunk-otel-collector` for cluster metrics
spl:
index=devops (sourcetype="helm:release" OR (sourcetype="fluxcd:controller" AND kind="HelmRelease"))
| where status IN ("failed","pending-upgrade","pending-rollback") OR info_status!="deployed"
| stats latest(_time) as last_event, values(message) as notes by release, namespace, chart, status
| sort -last_event
description: Failed or pending Helm releases leave workloads partially updated or broken; monitoring release status prevents silent partial deploys.
value: Failed or pending Helm releases leave workloads partially updated or broken; monitoring release status prevents silent partial deploys.
implementation: Ingest Helm release status from `helm list -o json` jobs, Flux HelmRelease conditions, or controller logs. Map statuses to deployed/failed/pending. Alert on non-deployed steady states. Include chart version and values hash for change correlation.
visualization: Table (unhealthy releases), Single value (non-deployed count), Timeline (release operations).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Helm CLI / Flux helm-controller logs, `splunk-otel-collector` for cluster metrics.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=helm:release`, `sourcetype=fluxcd:controller`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Ingest Helm release status from `helm list -o json` jobs, Flux HelmRelease conditions, or controller logs. Map statuses to deployed/failed/pending. Alert on non-deployed steady states. Include chart version and values hash for change correlation.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=devops (sourcetype="helm:release" OR (sourcetype="fluxcd:controller" AND kind="HelmRelease"))
| where status IN ("failed","pending-upgrade","pending-rollback") OR info_status!="deployed"
| stats latest(_time) as last_event, values(message) as notes by release, namespace, chart, status
| sort -last_event
```

Understanding this SPL

**Helm Release Health Monitoring** — Failed or pending Helm releases leave workloads partially updated or broken; monitoring release status prevents silent partial deploys.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=helm:release`, `sourcetype=fluxcd:controller`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Helm CLI / Flux helm-controller logs, `splunk-otel-collector` for cluster metrics. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: devops; **sourcetype**: helm:release, fluxcd:controller. Those sourcetypes align with what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=devops, sourcetype="helm:release". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Filters the current rows with `where status IN ("failed","pending-upgrade","pending-rollback") OR info_status!="deployed"` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by release, namespace, chart, status** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with Flux Kustomize/Helm status and source controller logs. Verify field extractions and index permissions.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (unhealthy releases), Single value (non-deployed count), Timeline (release operations).
grandmaExplanation: We watch Kubernetes release tooling so manifest or chart problems surface before users do.
knownFalsePositives: Short-lived sync issues during cluster upgrades, credential rotations, or Git provider incidents.
